title: Investors' Conference: Was It Not an Election-Time Sleight of Hand?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. The eight source pages were checked for company names, conference dates, announced and realised investment figures, percentages, quoted headlines, and page joins. Only visually clear OCR character and word-boundary errors were corrected in this translation file.
paragraphs: There is a village proverb about letting go of the quarry and clutching at its tail. In just that way, after sleeping soundly for four years, they have suddenly awakened, announced that they would convene a Global Investors Meet and amass investments, and staged some kind of drama.

Every newspaper that wrote about the conference also wrote about the AIADMK's dance and revelry surrounding it, the traffic congestion across the city, and the public's murmurs of irritation. A few years ago, the AIADMK Government similarly summoned investors and mounted a great publicity campaign, claiming that agreements worth many hundreds of crores had been signed and that hundreds of people would gain employment. But no investment arrived and no jobs were created. When even those earlier Memoranda of Understanding were never properly implemented, can agreements signed casually by an AIADMK Government on its way out possess any value?

That is why even the industrial investors attending now have spoken with great caution, saying that they will invest so many crores over the next three years, or plan to invest so many crores over the next five years. Although the Government advertised that investors from many countries were attending the Global Investors Meet, no foreign investor appears to have spoken at its opening ceremony.
Let me briefly recount what the AIADMK Government did about industrial investment during the past four and a half years, and what results followed.

On 13 February 2014, O. Panneerselvam, reading the AIADMK Government's Budget in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly, said on page 31: “I am happy to inform this august House that, to further promote industrial growth and infrastructure in our State, this Government will hold a Global Investors Meet in Chennai this coming October. ₹100 crore has been allotted for it in this Budget.” That was an announcement read in last year's Budget. But was the Investors Meet held in October as the Minister told the Assembly? Not at all. Such was the fate of an announcement made in the Budget itself!

When questioned about this, they point to their colossal announcement in 2012 that MoUs would be signed with twelve companies in a single day, that 36,855 people would receive jobs, and that factories would be started with an investment of ₹20,925 crore. On another occasion, they announced agreements with sixteen companies worth ₹5,081 crore. What became of all those announcements? They vanished into the air. Did all twelve companies establish factories as promised? Did the assured investment arrive?

Later, forgetting every earlier announcement, they newly declared that MoUs had been signed for a total investment of ₹26,625 crore, that investments worth ₹10,660 crore had consequently been made, and that 10,022 additional jobs had been created.

After that, Panneerselvam announced that the Investors Meet would be held in May 2015. Was even that held? No. Speaking in Coimbatore, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said that during her three years in office, MoUs for investment worth ₹31,706 crore had been concluded with thirty-three Indian and multinational companies. MoUs can be signed with any number of companies, but the State benefits only when industries actually begin operating.
What is the true position? Do you know what the general-interest weekly Ananda Vikatan, not I, wrote?

“The expansion of Hyundai's car factory operating in Tamil Nadu may be established in Rajasthan or Gujarat. Isuzu has gone to Andhra Pradesh. Saint-Gobain's ₹1,000-crore glass-manufacturing expansion plant, from a company operating in Chennai, has gone to Rajasthan. Tamil Nadu's own Ramco Cements has obtained permission to establish in Andhra Pradesh a factory producing 2.5 million tonnes of cement annually. The Industries Department has failed to concentrate even on efforts to retain them in Tamil Nadu.

“Statistics indicate that industrial investment in Tamil Nadu has fallen by approximately ₹50,000 crore over the last four years alone. With numerous obstacles persisting in the industrial sector, both multinational and domestic companies hesitate to invest in Tamil Nadu. The Industries Department lies exhausted, without even the ability to hold a conference to attract those investors towards the State. Industrialists cannot even meet the Industries Minister. Because power cuts, administrative complications, and rising costs mean Tamil Nadu lacks a favourable environment for industrial investment, many multinational companies are trying to move their factories to other States.”
Thus did Ananda Vikatan issue its certificate to the Jayalalithaa Government's Industries Department.

Foxconn, which operated in Tamil Nadu, has signed an agreement worth ₹32,000 crore to move to Maharashtra. Were even the industries already operating in Tamil Nadu retained? Thoothukudi's SPIC and Madras Fertilizers, both already operating here, are at risk of closure. Tamil Nadu Petroproducts, Trichy Distilleries, and Thoothukudi Alkali Chemicals have already closed. Work began three years ago on the Cuddalore Nagarjuna Petrochemical industrial complex with investment running into many thousands of crores, yet no one knows when it will begin production.

The textile-mill industry is weakening under many problems. The firecracker industry is being crushed by indirect encroachment. The handloom and power-loom industries are shrinking and curling in upon themselves. Tamil Nadu is living out the story of abandoning the grain in one's hand while trying to cook and feast upon false grain!

For the conference scheduled for May, they mounted enormous publicity claiming that agreements worth ₹18,000 crore with seven companies would be signed at a preview meeting held in Chennai on 13 February 2015. Yet that signing did not occur at the preview meeting at all. Is that not shameful?

Tamil Nadu's Industries Minister openly said: “They invite companies to Andhra Pradesh by promising cheap land and tax concessions. Likewise, whenever the Andhra Pradesh Chief Minister passes through Chennai, he calls our industrialists and tells them he will provide land cheaply.” That statement itself revealed Tamil Nadu's condition. When the Chief Minister of another State enters a State governed by these people, holds a meeting, and directly invites its industrial investors away, this Government ought to feel ashamed.

I am not inventing the claim that the Karnataka Chief Minister held a meeting in Coimbatore and invited Tamil Nadu's industrial investors. Dinamalar is not a DMK publication; it is a newspaper that harshly criticises the DMK. On 22 January it carried a report headlined: “Tamil Nadu Pushes Away; Karnataka Attracts: ₹12,000 Crore Investment Slips from Our Hands.”

In this situation, there was a report that Panneerselvam held talks with the United Kingdom's Trade Minister about British companies investing more extensively in Tamil Nadu, and likewise with the Governor of South Carolina in the United States about further investment here. No one knows what became of that either.

They announced that the Global Investors Meet would take place on 23 and 24 May. A campaign said officials were going abroad to attract foreigners to the conference, and reports appeared that officials had descended upon foreign countries in force. Tamil Nadu's Industries Minister said that numerous industrialists from abroad would attend the Chennai conference and that it offered the prospect of ₹1 lakh crore being invested in Tamil Nadu. Within days of his announcement, the Government announced that the Investors Meet had been postponed again.

A consultation meeting was reportedly held in Salem to invite local industrialists to the Global Investors Meet. The Industries Minister, Highways Minister, and Industries Department Special Secretary attended it, and the Ministers spoke there of the conference being held in May. Yet without even their knowledge, an announcement came from Chennai that it had been postponed until September.

An issue of Kanini published extensive details under a headline telling readers not to be intimidated by the continuing reports that astonishing numbers of investors and many thousands of crores in investment would come to Tamil Nadu through the Global Investors Meet. The Times of India says an agreement for ₹1 lakh crore in investment will be reached. The New Indian Express says agreements worth more than ₹1 lakh crore will be signed in the electricity sector alone and that the total will exceed ₹2 lakh crore.

When not even a single paisa has arrived from these greatly exaggerated investments, reports continue to magnify the mere signing of agreements on paper. How will these MoUs be implemented? When will implementation begin? Has any deadline been fixed? Do the Tamil Nadu Government and the investors signing them possess the basic resources needed to honour their commitments? Will the Government's industrial policy and its officials cooperate and permit industrial projects to be implemented without the slightest delay? Such questions naturally arise in the mind of everyone reading these reports.

The most important question is this: how many of the MoUs being signed will truly be implemented? Before seeking an answer, we must understand what progress Tamil Nadu made in industrial investment during the past four years.

In 2011, reports said MoUs worth ₹73,298 crore had been signed in Tamil Nadu. Yet only ₹235 crore in investment actually reached the State. Everyone should feel ashamed to learn that the realised investment was just 0.3 percent.
In 2012, MoUs worth ₹21,253 crore were signed, but only ₹524 crore was implemented: just 2.4 percent.
In 2013, agreements worth ₹27,380 crore were signed, but only ₹2,292 crore in investment arrived: 8.3 percent.
In 2014, agreements worth ₹14,596 crore were signed, but only ₹2,500 crore reached Tamil Nadu: 17 percent.

Up to July 2015, agreements worth ₹17,412 crore were signed, but work representing a mere ₹41 crore in investment was completed. The realised investment was only 0.2 percent, enough to make anyone hang their head.

Thus, from 2011 through July 2015 under AIADMK rule, only 5.64 percent of the promised investment was implemented. The enormous gap between investment covered by MoUs and investment actually realised exists because basic infrastructure, electricity, water, and transport, is not available in the required quantity or quality, and because the Government's industrial policy and administrative machinery do not adopt an investor-friendly approach.

For four and a half years, they followed no suitable method for attracting industrial investment and suffered a massive failure. After all that, how feasible is their last-minute boast that they will now achieve great things as publicity for the 2016 election? What beneficial results can it possibly produce?
With affection,
M.K.
11 September 2015
